HEADLINE: By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S
SOURCE: Newsweek

ARTICLE:

By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S. President Donald Trump has said the U.S. is "seeking immediate negotiations" to acquire Greenland, deepening the yawning chasm opening up between Washington and its closest allies over the Arctic territory.Trump called Greenland "part of North America" and "our territory" during a rambling speech to world leaders at the World Economic Forum in the Swiss town of Davos on Wednesday.The Republican reiterated his claim that the U.S. is the only power that can protect the mostly ice-covered expanse, and insisted this would not pose a threat to NATO.Alliance officials have repeatedly said a U.S. military attack on Greenland, a semi-autonomous part of Denmark, would spell the end of NATO. The prospect of the U.S. — the most influential and powerful member of NATO —attacking territory belonging to a fellow alliance member has always been unfathomable, until now.The White House had refused to rule out using military force to take Greenland, which would be a violation of international law."I don't want to use force. I won't use force," Trump said. "All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland."This is a breaking story.
